questions.question: The doctrine of restraint of trade renders certain contractual restrictions on a party's freedom to trade void unless they can be justified. What is the starting presumption under the doctrine?

options.A: All restraints of trade are valid unless shown to be unreasonable
options.B: All restraints of trade are prima facie void — they will only be enforceable if justified by the covenantee
options.C: Only post-employment restraints are subject to the doctrine; covenants in commercial agreements are presumptively valid
options.D: The doctrine applies only where the restrained party is in a weaker bargaining position

questions.explanation: The starting point of the restraint of trade doctrine is that all restraints of trade are prima facie void and contrary to public policy. The covenantee (the party seeking to enforce the restraint) must justify the restraint by demonstrating: (1) a legitimate proprietary interest worthy of protection; (2) that the restraint is reasonable as between the parties (not wider than necessary to protect that interest); and (3) that the restraint is not contrary to the public interest. This was confirmed by the House of Lords in Nordenfelt v Maxim Nordenfelt Guns and Ammunition Co [1894].

questions.question: In Nordenfelt v Maxim Nordenfelt Guns and Ammunition Co [1894] (HL), the House of Lords established the foundational test for restraint of trade. The test requires the restraint to be:

options.A: Reasonable in its scope, area, and duration, and not contrary to the public interest
options.B: Necessary to prevent direct competition in the specific geographic area covered
options.C: Proportionate to the financial consideration paid to the covenantor
options.D: Confined to a period of no more than two years from the date the restriction begins

questions.explanation: In Nordenfelt [1894], the House of Lords held that a restraint of trade is enforceable if it is reasonable — reasonableness is assessed from two perspectives: (1) as between the parties — is the restraint no wider than is reasonably necessary to protect the covenantee's legitimate interest?; and (2) in terms of the public interest — does the restraint not harm the public by unduly restricting competition? Both limbs must be satisfied. In Nordenfelt itself, a worldwide restraint lasting 25 years was upheld because the business was genuinely global and the consideration (a large sum) was adequate.

questions.question: In the context of post-employment restraints, which of the following is recognised as a legitimate proprietary interest that an employer may protect?

options.A: The employer's general interest in preventing competition from a former employee
options.B: Trade secrets and confidential information, and customer connections (goodwill)
options.C: The employer's investment in training the employee
options.D: The employer's workforce stability and morale

questions.explanation: The courts recognise only limited categories of legitimate proprietary interest in post-employment restraints: (1) trade secrets and genuinely confidential information; and (2) customer connections or goodwill (where the employee had close personal contact with clients and could exploit those relationships). The employer's general desire to prevent competition or to protect investment in training is not, by itself, a sufficient proprietary interest to justify a post-employment restraint: Herbert Morris Ltd v Saxelby [1916] (HL). A bare non-competition clause protecting only the employer's general business interest will not be upheld.

questions.question: In Esso Petroleum Co Ltd v Harper's Garage (Stourport) Ltd [1968] (HL), the House of Lords considered solus (exclusive dealing) agreements. The Court applied the restraint of trade doctrine and held that:

options.A: Exclusive dealing agreements are automatically void as they restrain a party's freedom to trade with others
options.B: A five-year exclusive dealing arrangement was valid but a 21-year arrangement was unreasonably long and void as a restraint of trade
options.C: The restraint of trade doctrine does not apply to commercial agreements between companies of equal bargaining power
options.D: Exclusive supply agreements in the fuel industry are exempt from the restraint of trade doctrine by statute

questions.explanation: In Esso Petroleum v Harper's Garage [1968], the House of Lords applied the restraint of trade doctrine to solus (exclusive dealing) petrol supply agreements. A five-year exclusive agreement (where the garage tied its sales exclusively to Esso) was held reasonable and enforceable. A second agreement of 21 years tied to a mortgage was held unreasonably long in its duration and therefore void as an unjustifiable restraint of trade. The case confirmed that the doctrine applies to commercial agreements, not just employment contracts.

questions.question: A covenant in restraint of trade must be reasonable 'as between the parties.' This means:

options.A: Both parties must agree in writing that the restraint is reasonable
options.B: The restraint must be no wider — in terms of activity restricted, geographic area, and duration — than is reasonably necessary to protect the covenantee's legitimate interest
options.C: Both parties must receive legal advice before the covenant is valid
options.D: The court must balance the financial consideration paid against the scope of the restraint

questions.explanation: Reasonableness as between the parties requires that the restraint is drafted no more widely than is necessary to protect the covenantee's identified legitimate interest. The three dimensions of reasonableness are: (1) scope of activity restricted — does it go beyond what threatens the legitimate interest?; (2) geographic area — does it extend beyond the area where the protected interest is at risk?; and (3) duration — is the time period longer than needed? A covenant that is over-broad in any of these dimensions will fail as an unreasonable restraint unless the courts can sever the excess.

questions.question: In the sale of a business context, a vendor sells their business together with its goodwill and provides a covenant not to compete for five years within a 50-mile radius. Such a covenant is:

options.A: Always void as it prevents the vendor from working
options.B: More readily enforceable than equivalent post-employment covenants, because the purchaser has paid for the goodwill and the vendor has agreed not to undermine it
options.C: Enforceable only if the purchaser can prove actual financial loss resulting from competition
options.D: Enforceable only for the period during which the purchase price has not been fully repaid

questions.explanation: Courts are more willing to uphold restraints in the sale of a business context than in employment contracts. Where a vendor sells a business including its goodwill, there is a clear legitimate interest (the goodwill purchased) and the parties are typically in equal bargaining positions. The vendor has received financial consideration specifically referable to the restriction and has agreed not to undermine the value they have sold. Accordingly, the courts will enforce wider restraints in business sale contexts than they would in employment: Nordenfelt [1894], Allied Dunbar v Weisinger [1988].

questions.question: The Irish case of Mulligan v Corr [1925] (SC Ireland) concerned a covenant in a partnership dissolution agreement. The Irish courts held that:

options.A: Partnership restraints are void as contrary to the principle of freedom of trade
options.B: A restraint in a partnership dissolution agreement is subject to the same test as commercial restraints generally — it will be enforced if it is reasonable in the interests of the parties and the public
options.C: Only restraints of less than three years are enforceable in partnership dissolution agreements under Irish law
options.D: The restraint of trade doctrine does not apply to partnerships — they are governed exclusively by the Partnership Act 1890

questions.explanation: In Mulligan v Corr [1925], the Irish Supreme Court confirmed that the restraint of trade doctrine applies to covenants in partnership dissolution agreements and assessed the covenant by reference to the reasonableness standard — whether it was reasonable in the interests of the parties and not contrary to the public interest. This confirms that Irish law applies the same principles as English law in this area, following the approach established in Nordenfelt [1894] and the English authorities on restraint of trade in commercial and professional contexts.

questions.question: The 'blue pencil' rule in the context of restraint of trade covenants allows the court to:

options.A: Rewrite an overly broad covenant to make it enforceable by substituting reasonable limits
options.B: Strike through and delete words from the covenant to remove the offending excess, provided the remainder is sensible and the deletion does not require adding or substituting words
options.C: Reduce the financial consideration paid if the covenant is found to be unreasonably broad
options.D: Divide the covenant into separate geographic zones and enforce it in some areas but not others

questions.explanation: The blue pencil rule allows courts to sever the excessive part of a restraint of trade covenant by literally striking through the offending words, provided the remainder makes grammatical and legal sense without requiring any additions or substitutions. In Goldsoll v Goldman [1915] (CA England), a clause prohibiting dealing anywhere in the world was blue-pencilled to remove the geographic excess, leaving a valid restriction in the UK and Ireland. The courts cannot rewrite or substitute words — they can only delete. If deletion would so alter the character of the covenant that it becomes a different obligation, severance will not be ordered.

questions.question: A non-solicitation clause in an employment contract that restricts a departing employee from soliciting former clients for 12 months after leaving:

options.A: Is automatically valid because 12 months is a reasonable period for any non-solicitation restraint
options.B: Will be assessed for reasonableness — it is more likely to be enforceable than a non-competition clause if it is limited to clients the employee personally dealt with
options.C: Is void because it prevents the employee from making a living in their profession
options.D: Requires statutory approval from the Workplace Relations Commission before it is enforceable

questions.explanation: Non-solicitation clauses are generally viewed more favourably by courts than outright non-competition clauses, because they are more narrowly targeted at protecting a specific legitimate interest (the employer's customer relationships). A non-solicitation clause limited to clients the employee personally dealt with during their employment, for a reasonable period, is likely to be enforced. The reasonableness of the period depends on the industry, the nature of the relationships, and how quickly goodwill is likely to be eroded without the employee's involvement.

questions.question: What is meant by 'legitimate proprietary interest' in the restraint of trade doctrine?

options.A: Any interest that is commercially valuable to the covenantee
options.B: A specific, identifiable interest recognised by law as worthy of protection beyond the employer's or covenantee's general interest in avoiding competition
options.C: An interest that is registered as intellectual property under Irish or EU law
options.D: Any interest that the parties have agreed is worth protecting in the contract

questions.explanation: A 'legitimate proprietary interest' is a specific category of interest that the law recognises as justifying a restriction on freedom to trade. The courts do not protect mere commercial interests in avoiding competition. Recognised categories include: trade secrets and confidential information (a property right); customer connections or goodwill in employment contexts (where the employee has personal relationships with clients); and the goodwill of a business that has been sold. Investment in training, staff stability, and general competitive advantage are not recognised legitimate proprietary interests: Herbert Morris Ltd v Saxelby [1916] (HL).

questions.question: In Tipperary Co-operative Creamery Ltd v Donoghue [1966] (HC Ireland), the Irish High Court considered a non-competition covenant in an employment context. The case illustrates which principle?

options.A: Irish courts apply a stricter test for post-employment restraints than the English common law standard
options.B: Irish courts apply the standard Nordenfelt reasonableness test to post-employment covenants and will strike down covenants that go beyond what is necessary to protect a legitimate proprietary interest
options.C: Non-competition covenants in Ireland require registration with the Companies Registration Office to be enforceable
options.D: The Irish courts treat post-employment non-competition covenants as automatically enforceable if agreed in a written employment contract

questions.explanation: Tipperary Co-operative Creamery Ltd v Donoghue [1966] confirms that Irish courts apply the Nordenfelt reasonableness standard to post-employment restraints. The High Court examined whether the employer had a legitimate proprietary interest, and whether the restraint in terms of activity, area, and duration was no wider than reasonably necessary to protect that interest. This follows the English approach: Herbert Morris v Saxelby [1916] and Mason v Provident Clothing [1913]. The Irish courts will strike down over-broad restraints that are designed to prevent competition rather than to protect a specific identifiable interest.

questions.question: A covenant restricting a former employee from working in the same industry anywhere in Ireland for three years is most likely to be:

options.A: Valid — three years and a whole country is a typical and enforceable scope for senior employees
options.B: Void as an unreasonable restraint — it is too broad in both geographic scope and duration unless the employee held a very senior role with access to highly sensitive confidential information
options.C: Valid because Ireland is a small country and any geographic restriction less than Ireland-wide would be commercially ineffective
options.D: Void because Irish constitutional law prohibits all restrictions on the right to work

questions.explanation: A nationwide restriction for three years would need to be justified by a very significant and identifiable proprietary interest. For most employees, even senior ones, such a broad geographic and temporal restriction is likely to be held unreasonable — going further than necessary to protect trade secrets or customer connections. The employer must show not only that they have a legitimate interest but that the specific scope of the restraint is proportionate to that interest. Courts are reluctant to enforce restrictions that are in substance designed to prevent competition rather than to protect specific proprietary rights.

questions.question: Solus agreements — where a party agrees to deal exclusively with one supplier — are subject to the restraint of trade doctrine because:

options.A: They are always anti-competitive and contrary to EU competition law
options.B: They restrict the covenantor's freedom to trade with others and therefore potentially attract the doctrine if they go beyond what is reasonable
options.C: They prevent third party suppliers from entering the market
options.D: They are classified as unfair terms under the Consumer Rights Act 2022

questions.explanation: Solus agreements restrict the covenantor's freedom to choose their trading partners — they commit the party to exclusive dealing with one supplier. The House of Lords in Esso Petroleum v Harper's Garage [1968] confirmed that such agreements attract the restraint of trade doctrine if they go beyond what is reasonable in the circumstances. The covenantee must show a legitimate interest in the exclusivity and that the period and scope of the tie is not wider than necessary. Very long exclusive ties are particularly vulnerable — the 21-year mortgage tie in Esso was void.

questions.question: In the context of commercial agreements between parties of equal bargaining power, the restraint of trade doctrine:

options.A: Does not apply — the doctrine is confined to employment contracts
options.B: Applies, but courts may give greater weight to the parties' freedom to contract and may be more willing to uphold the restraint where the consideration given is substantial
options.C: Automatically validates the restraint — equal bargaining power proves reasonableness
options.D: Applies more strictly than in employment contexts — commercial parties are held to higher standards

questions.explanation: The restraint of trade doctrine applies to commercial agreements between companies, not only to employment contracts. However, courts are generally more willing to enforce restraints in commercial contexts (particularly in the sale of a business) where the parties negotiated at arm's length, were of comparable bargaining power, and the consideration reflects the value of the restriction. The doctrine still applies and the restraint must be justifiable, but the courts give greater latitude to commercial bargaining: Nordenfelt [1894], Esso v Harper's Garage [1968].

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a relevant factor in assessing the reasonableness of a restraint of trade clause?

options.B: The geographic area covered by the restraint
options.C: The activities or types of work restricted
options.D: The financial turnover of the covenantee's business in the previous year

questions.explanation: The factors relevant to reasonableness in restraint of trade are: (1) duration — is the time period longer than necessary?; (2) geographic scope — is the area covered broader than where the protected interest exists?; and (3) scope of restricted activities — does the restraint go beyond the specific activities that threaten the legitimate interest? The financial turnover of the covenantee's business is not itself a relevant factor in the reasonableness assessment, though the size and nature of the business may be contextually relevant in assessing what geographic or activity scope is necessary.

questions.question: A garden leave clause in an employment contract requires an employee who has resigned to remain off work (on full pay) for a specified period before leaving. Such clauses:

options.A: Are void restraints of trade because they prevent the employee from working
options.B: Are generally valid and are an alternative to traditional post-termination restraints — the employer's interest is protected by keeping the employee on salary and away from competitors during the notice period
options.C: Are automatically void in Ireland under the Employment Equality Acts
options.D: Are enforceable only where they do not exceed three months in duration

questions.explanation: Garden leave provisions — where an employee is required to remain at home on full pay during their notice period, without doing any work or having access to clients or confidential information — are generally valid. They serve the same protective function as post-termination restraints but with the advantage that the employee remains employed (and bound by implied duties of fidelity) and is being paid. The validity of garden leave is recognised in English and Irish employment law as a legitimate way of protecting the employer's interests during the transition period.

questions.question: In the Irish case of John Orr Ltd v Orr [1987] (HC Ireland), the High Court considered a restraint on a former managing director. The Court held the restraint void because:

options.A: The former employee was the majority shareholder and therefore not bound by employment law obligations
options.B: The restraint extended to activities that went beyond protecting any legitimate proprietary interest of the employer and was too wide in its scope
options.C: Managing directors cannot be restrained from working for competitors under Irish employment law
options.D: The restraint had been agreed without consideration flowing from the employer

questions.explanation: In John Orr Ltd v Orr [1987], the Irish High Court applied the Nordenfelt reasonableness standard and found the restraint of trade covenant to be unreasonably wide — it went beyond what was necessary to protect any legitimate proprietary interest of the employer. The Court was not satisfied that the employer had identified a sufficiently specific interest to justify the breadth of the restriction. The case is a useful Irish authority confirming that even senior employees' covenants must satisfy the reasonableness test and will be struck down if over-broad.

questions.question: What is the relationship between the common law doctrine of restraint of trade and EU competition law (Articles 101–102 TFEU)?

options.A: EU competition law replaces the common law doctrine — the restraint of trade doctrine has no application in Ireland since accession to the EU
options.B: Both the common law doctrine and EU competition law may apply concurrently — a covenant may be void under the restraint of trade doctrine and also prohibited under Article 101 TFEU if it appreciably restricts competition
options.C: EU competition law applies only to agreements between undertakings in different Member States — domestic Irish restraints are governed only by the common law
options.D: The common law doctrine of restraint of trade is stricter than EU competition law and thus always prevails

questions.explanation: Both the common law restraint of trade doctrine and EU competition law (Articles 101–102 TFEU and their Irish equivalents under the Competition Act 2002) may apply to restrictive agreements. Article 101 TFEU prohibits agreements between undertakings that appreciably restrict competition within the EU; this may capture restraint of trade clauses in commercial agreements between businesses. EU competition law operates separately from and in addition to the common law doctrine — a clause may fail under either or both. Irish competition law (Competition Act 2002) also applies domestically alongside the EU framework.
